author: Robert Carr
description: A double smash burger with cold 80/20 beef, a thin crisp griddle crust, shaved onion smashed into the patties, melted American cheese, a toasted potato bun, dill pickles, crisp lettuce, and homemade burger sauce.
equipment: Cast-iron griddle or large cast-iron skillet
Heavy metal spatula or burger press
ingredients: 6 ounces cold 80/20 ground beef, preferably freshly ground chuck or a chuck and short rib blend
1 small yellow onion, 1 teaspoon very finely grated for sauce and enough of the rest shaved paper-thin for the patties
1 teaspoon beef tallow or neutral oil, optional
1/4 teaspoon Louisiana-style cayenne hot sauce, such as Frank's RedHot, Crystal, or Texas Pete
1/2 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt or 1/4 teaspoon Morton kosher salt, for seasoning the patties
instructions: Grate 1 teaspoon from the yellow onion into a small bowl for the sauce, then shave enough of the remaining onion paper-thin to loosely cover 2 patties.
Spread the shaved onion on a paper towel-lined plate and pat very dry. Wet onion steams the beef instead of browning with it.

Make the burger sauce by stirring together 2 tablespoons mayonnaise, 1 1/2 teaspoons ketchup, 1 teaspoon yellow mustard, 1 teaspoon pickle brine, the 1 teaspoon very finely grated onion and any onion juice, 1/4 teaspoon Worcestershire sauce, 1/4 teaspoon Louisiana-style cayenne hot sauce, a pinch smoked paprika, and a pinch garlic powder.
Cover and refrigerate the sauce for at least 15 minutes while preparing the burger so the onion, pickle brine, and spices season the mayonnaise.
Mix 1/2 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, 1/4 teaspoon black pepper, and a pinch MSG if using in a small bowl. This is the patty seasoning.
Divide 6 ounces cold ground beef into 2 loose 3-ounce portions.
Gently shape each portion into a rough ball without packing the meat tightly.
Place the beef balls on a sheet pan and refrigerate for 15 minutes while preparing the bun and preheating the pan.
Spread the 1/2 tablespoon softened butter evenly over the cut sides of the potato bun.

Preheat a cast-iron griddle or large cast-iron skillet over medium-high to high heat for 5 to 8 minutes, until a drop of water skitters and evaporates quickly or an infrared thermometer reads about 425 degrees F to 475 degrees F.
Toast the buttered bun cut-side down until golden brown, then set it aside.
After the bun is toasted, return the pan to medium-high to high heat for 1 minute so it recovers before the beef goes in.
If the cooking surface is dry, add 1 teaspoon beef tallow or neutral oil and spread it into a thin film with the spatula.
Cook the 2 beef balls together only if the pan is wide enough for both patties to have room.

Place each beef ball on the hot pan, immediately cover with a parchment square, and smash very hard with a burger press or heavy metal spatula for 8 to 10 seconds, until the patty is about 1/4 inch thick with ragged edges.
Remove the parchment and season the top of each patty with half of the salt, pepper, and MSG mixture, using all of the seasoning across the 2 patties.
Scatter half of the shaved onion over the raw top of each smashed patty.
Cook without moving for 90 seconds to 2 minutes, until the edges are browned and crisp.
Use a bench scraper or stiff spatula to scrape firmly under each patty so the crust stays attached to the beef.
Flip each patty onion-side down.
Immediately place 1 slice American cheese on each patty.
Cook for 45 to 75 seconds more, until the onions brown underneath, the cheese melts, and the beef is cooked through to 160 degrees F if checking with an instant-read thermometer.
Stack the 2 patties together for the burger.
Spread 1 1/2 tablespoons burger sauce on the toasted bottom bun.
Add 1/2 cup shredded lettuce and 1/4 cup dill pickle chips to the bottom bun.
Place the double stack of smash patties on the prepared bottom bun.
Spread 1 tablespoon burger sauce on the toasted top bun and serve any remaining sauce on the side.
Close the burger and serve immediately while the edges are still crisp.
notes: The big flavor moves are cold 80/20 beef, a ripping-hot pan, an immediate hard smash, dry shaved onion, American cheese, pickles, and a sauce with pickle brine and grated onion.
Do not mix salt into the ground beef before shaping. Salting the inside can make the patties tighter and more sausage-like.
Smash only once, right after the beef hits the pan. Pressing later squeezes out fat and juices.
A loosely packed 3-ounce ball gives the best ratio of crispy crust to juicy middle for a double burger.
American cheese is intentional here because it melts fast enough to glue the two thin patties together.
Potato buns are ideal because they are soft, slightly sweet, and sturdy enough for sauce and beef fat.

Use a thin Louisiana-style cayenne hot sauce, such as Frank's RedHot, Crystal, or Texas Pete. Avoid smoky chipotle hot sauce, sweet hot sauce, fermented hot sauce, or extra-hot sauces here because they can overpower the burger sauce.
If the onions look wet after slicing, pat them dry again before cooking.
If the pan starts smoking aggressively or the onions burn before the cheese melts, lower the heat slightly for the next batch.
If the patties gray before they crust, the pan is too cool or too crowded.
Use a sharp-edged metal spatula or bench scraper. A flexible silicone spatula will not scrape the crust cleanly from the pan.
For a classic burger, skip the shaved onions and cook the patties plain. The onion smash version has more diner-style onion flavor.
For USDA guidance, cook ground beef to 160 degrees F. These thin patties cook very quickly, so the main quality risk is overcooking after the crust has already formed.
Serve immediately. Smash burgers lose the magic as the lacy edges steam inside the bun.
personalNotes: Good burgers, there will be a lot of left over burger sauce. Also the burgers will be very messy, but they are good.
title: Double Onion Smash Burger
totalTime: 30 minutes plus 15 minutes beef chill time
